question: What are the four pillars of OOP and how does Java implement them?
summary: Encapsulation, Inheritance, Polymorphism, Abstraction with Java examples.
whatTheyTest: Foundational OOP knowledge and ability to relate concepts to Java syntax.
explanation: Encapsulation: bundling data and methods together and restricting direct access using private fields + public getters/setters. Inheritance: a class extends another to reuse and extend behaviour (extends keyword). Polymorphism: one interface, many implementations — method overriding (runtime) and overloading (compile-time). Abstraction: hiding implementation details via abstract classes and interfaces, exposing only what callers need.
strongAnswerPoints: Encapsulation uses private fields + public accessors to protect state.
Inheritance enables code reuse but Java supports only single class inheritance.
Polymorphism at runtime uses dynamic dispatch — JVM calls the overriding method based on the actual object type.
Abstraction via interfaces allows dependency inversion — code to contracts, not implementations.
whatToAvoid: Don't just recite definitions — give concrete Java examples for each pillar.
ytQuery: four pillars of OOP Java explained
quickAnswer: Encapsulation (private fields + getters/setters), Inheritance (extends), Polymorphism (overriding/overloading), Abstraction (interfaces/abstract classes). Java enforces single inheritance for classes but allows multiple interface implementation.
eli10: OOP is like building with Lego. Each Lego piece hides how it's made inside (encapsulation), pieces can be built on top of others (inheritance), the same piece can snap into different sets (polymorphism), and you just see the outside shape not the plastic formula inside (abstraction).
realWorldExample: At Razorpay, the Payment interface abstracts over UPI, Card, and NetBanking implementations. New payment methods are added without touching existing code — that's abstraction + polymorphism working together.

question: What is the difference between abstract class and interface in Java? When do you use which?
summary: Abstract class vs interface — state, constructors, default methods, use cases.
whatTheyTest: Understanding of Java's type system and design decision-making.
explanation: Abstract class: can have state (instance variables), constructors, concrete and abstract methods. Supports single inheritance only. Use when classes share common state or behaviour. Interface: no instance state (only public static final constants), no constructors. From Java 8: can have default and static methods. From Java 9: private methods. A class can implement multiple interfaces. Use for defining contracts/capabilities.
strongAnswerPoints: Abstract class allows instance variables and constructors; interfaces do not.
A class can implement multiple interfaces but extend only one abstract class.
Java 8 added default methods to interfaces, blurring the line — but interfaces still can't hold state.
Use abstract class for IS-A with shared state; use interface for CAN-DO capabilities.
whatToAvoid: Don't say 'use interface when you have no implementation' — that's outdated since Java 8 default methods.
ytQuery: abstract class vs interface Java 8 difference
quickAnswer: Abstract class: single inheritance, can have state/constructors, partial implementation. Interface: multiple implementation, no state, pure contract (with Java 8 defaults). Use abstract class for shared state among related classes; use interface for defining capabilities across unrelated classes.
eli10: Interface is like a job description — it says what you must do. Abstract class is like a job training manual — it gives you some tools and says 'figure out the rest yourself'.
realWorldExample: Spring's HttpServlet uses abstract class (shared request/response state). Serializable, Runnable, and Comparable are interfaces — unrelated classes can all implement them without sharing a parent.

question: What is the difference between method overloading and method overriding?
summary: Compile-time vs runtime polymorphism in Java.
whatTheyTest: Understanding of polymorphism — compile-time vs runtime dispatch.
explanation: Overloading: same method name, different parameter list (type, count, or order) in the same class. Resolved at compile time. Return type alone cannot differentiate overloaded methods. Overriding: subclass provides its own implementation of a method declared in the parent class. Same name, same signature. Resolved at runtime via dynamic dispatch. Must use @Override annotation. Cannot override static, final, or private methods.
strongAnswerPoints: Overloading = compile-time polymorphism; overriding = runtime polymorphism.
Overloading differs by parameter list; overriding must match the parent's exact signature.
@Override annotation is not required but strongly recommended — catches mistakes at compile time.
Static methods can be hidden (not overridden) — no dynamic dispatch.
whatToAvoid: Don't confuse return type with method signature for overloading — changing only the return type causes a compile error.
ytQuery: method overloading vs overriding Java
quickAnswer: Overloading: same name, different parameters, resolved at compile time. Overriding: subclass redefines parent method with same signature, resolved at runtime via dynamic dispatch.
eli10: Overloading is like having one chef who can make a pizza small, medium, or large depending on what you order. Overriding is like a restaurant replacing the original chef's pizza recipe with their own signature version.
realWorldExample: Java's PrintStream has 10+ overloaded println() methods for int, String, Object etc. Collections.sort() is overridden differently in ArrayList vs LinkedList for optimal performance.

question: What does the `final` keyword mean when applied to a class, method, and variable?
summary: final prevents inheritance, overriding, and reassignment respectively.
whatTheyTest: Understanding of Java's immutability and restriction mechanisms.
explanation: final class: cannot be subclassed. String is final — ensures immutability and security. final method: cannot be overridden in subclasses. final variable: a primitive cannot be reassigned; a reference variable cannot point to a different object (but the object's internals can still change). Blank final: declared without init, must be assigned in constructor. Static final = compile-time constant.
strongAnswerPoints: final class prevents subclassing — String, Integer, and all wrapper classes are final.
final method prevents overriding but the class can still be subclassed.
final reference variable can't be reassigned, but the referenced object remains mutable.
static final fields are compile-time constants inlined by the compiler.
whatToAvoid: Don't confuse final reference = immutable object. The reference is fixed, not the object it points to.
ytQuery: Java final keyword class method variable explained
quickAnswer: final class: no subclassing. final method: no overriding. final variable: no reassignment (primitive value locked; reference locked but object mutable). String is final for immutability and thread safety.
eli10: final is like a 'do not change' sticker. On a class it means no copying allowed. On a method it means no customizing. On a variable it means once you write the value, it's permanent.
realWorldExample: Java's String is final to prevent malicious subclasses from overriding equals() or hashCode(), which would break HashMap lookups and security checks throughout the JDK.

question: What is the `static` keyword in Java? Explain static methods, variables, blocks, and nested classes.
summary: static belongs to the class, not instances — shared across all objects.
whatTheyTest: Understanding of class-level vs instance-level members.
explanation: static members belong to the class, not any specific instance. Static variable: one copy shared across all objects. Static method: can be called without an instance; cannot access instance variables or 'this'. Static block: runs once when the class is loaded, used for complex static initialization. Static nested class: defined inside another class but doesn't need an outer instance — unlike inner classes.
strongAnswerPoints: static variables are shared — one memory location for all instances.
static methods cannot use 'this' or access non-static members directly.
Static blocks are useful for loading drivers, initializing constants from config.
Static nested classes don't hold a reference to the outer class — lighter than inner classes.
whatToAvoid: Don't say static methods 'belong to objects' — they belong to the class. Also don't overuse static — it makes testing harder.
ytQuery: static keyword Java methods blocks nested class
quickAnswer: static = class-level, shared across instances. Static variables: one copy. Static methods: no 'this', no instance access. Static blocks: run once on class load. Static nested class: no outer instance reference.
eli10: static is like a notice board in school. Everyone (all objects) shares the same board. You don't need to create a student to read the notice board — it's always there for the whole class.
realWorldExample: Math.sqrt(), Collections.sort() are static utility methods. Logger.getLogger() uses a static factory. JDBC's Class.forName() uses a static block in the driver class to register itself.

question: How do you create an immutable class in Java?
summary: Immutable class: final class, private final fields, no setters, defensive copies.
whatTheyTest: Defensive programming, thread safety, and Java immutability patterns.
explanation: Rules for immutable class: 1) Declare class as final. 2) All fields private and final. 3) No setters. 4) Initialize all fields via constructor. 5) For mutable fields (arrays, collections, Date), return defensive copies in getters and accept defensive copies in constructor. Java's String, Integer, LocalDate are immutable. Immutable objects are inherently thread-safe.
strongAnswerPoints: final class prevents subclasses from adding mutability.
Defensive copies prevent external code from modifying internal mutable state.
Immutable objects are automatically thread-safe — no synchronization needed.
Java 16+ records are immutable by default: record Point(int x, int y) {}
whatToAvoid: Forgetting defensive copies for mutable fields like Date, arrays, or collections breaks immutability even if fields are final.
ytQuery: how to create immutable class Java
quickAnswer: final class, private final fields, no setters, set all fields in constructor, return defensive copies of mutable fields. Java 16+ records handle this automatically.
eli10: Immutable is like a book that's already printed. You can read it all you want but you can't change any words. If someone asks for a copy, you give them a photocopy — they can't change your original.
realWorldExample: Razorpay's transaction objects are immutable — once created, the amount, currency, and order ID cannot change. This eliminates entire classes of bugs and makes concurrent processing safe without locks.

question: What is the difference between ArrayList and LinkedList in Java?
summary: ArrayList: O(1) get, O(n) insert/delete. LinkedList: O(n) get, O(1) insert/delete at known node.
whatTheyTest: Data structure knowledge and time complexity trade-offs.
explanation: ArrayList: backed by a dynamic array. Random access is O(1) via index. Inserting/deleting in the middle is O(n) — elements must shift. Grows by 50% when capacity exceeded. Good for read-heavy workloads. LinkedList: doubly linked list. No random access — O(n) traversal to reach index. Inserting/deleting at head or tail is O(1), in the middle O(n) to find the node. Higher memory overhead (two pointers per node). Also implements Deque — can be used as a queue or stack.
strongAnswerPoints: ArrayList get(i) is O(1); LinkedList get(i) is O(n) — must traverse from head.
ArrayList insert/delete in middle is O(n) due to shifting; LinkedList is O(1) once you have the node.
ArrayList has better cache performance — contiguous memory vs scattered nodes.
In practice, ArrayList outperforms LinkedList for most use cases due to cache locality.
whatToAvoid: Don't say LinkedList is always better for insertions — you still need O(n) to find the position. Only head/tail ops are O(1).
ytQuery: ArrayList vs LinkedList Java performance difference
quickAnswer: ArrayList: O(1) random access, O(n) insert/delete. LinkedList: O(n) access, O(1) insert/delete at ends. ArrayList preferred for most cases due to cache locality. Use LinkedList as a Deque/Queue.
eli10: ArrayList is a row of chairs numbered 1-100. You can jump to chair 50 instantly. But adding a chair in the middle means moving all others. LinkedList is like a treasure hunt — each clue points to the next. Easy to add clues anywhere, but you always start from the beginning.
realWorldExample: Swiggy's order queue uses LinkedList (as Deque) for O(1) enqueue/dequeue. The order history list uses ArrayList for fast indexed access when displaying past orders.

question: How does HashMap work internally in Java? Explain hashCode, equals, buckets, and load factor.
summary: HashMap uses array of buckets + linked lists/trees. hashCode finds bucket, equals resolves collisions.
whatTheyTest: Deep understanding of the most commonly used data structure in Java.
explanation: HashMap stores entries as key-value pairs in an array of buckets (default 16). To put a key: 1) Call key.hashCode() → index = hash & (capacity-1). 2) If bucket empty, store there. 3) If collision (same bucket), iterate the chain using equals() to find if key exists (update) or append. From Java 8, chains > 8 elements convert to a Red-Black Tree (O(log n) instead of O(n)). Load factor (default 0.75): when size > capacity × 0.75, rehash — new array 2x bigger, all entries reindexed. get() is O(1) average, O(log n) worst case (tree bucket).
strongAnswerPoints: hashCode() determines the bucket index; equals() resolves which entry in that bucket.
If two objects are equal (equals() = true), they MUST have the same hashCode.
Java 8 converts bucket chains > 8 to Red-Black Trees for O(log n) worst case.
Always override both hashCode and equals together — never just one.
whatToAvoid: Don't say HashMap is always O(1) — with many collisions it degrades. Don't use mutable fields in hashCode — changing a key after insertion loses the entry.
ytQuery: HashMap internal working Java hashCode equals buckets
quickAnswer: HashMap uses array of buckets. hashCode() → bucket index. equals() → exact key match within bucket. Java 8 uses trees for long chains. Load factor 0.75 triggers rehashing at 75% capacity.
eli10: HashMap is like a post office with 16 mailboxes. Your name's hash decides which mailbox. If two people share a mailbox, the postman reads the name label (equals) to find your mail. If a mailbox gets too full, they build a bigger post office and redistribute all the mail.
realWorldExample: At PhonePe, session tokens are stored in a HashMap<String, UserSession>. A poorly implemented hashCode that always returns 0 would cause all sessions to land in one bucket — O(n) lookup instead of O(1), crashing under load.

question: What is the difference between HashMap, LinkedHashMap, and TreeMap?
summary: HashMap: no order. LinkedHashMap: insertion order. TreeMap: sorted by key.
whatTheyTest: Choosing the right Map implementation for ordering requirements.
explanation: HashMap: no guaranteed iteration order. O(1) get/put average. LinkedHashMap: extends HashMap, maintains a doubly linked list of entries in insertion order (or access order if specified). Slightly higher memory. O(1) operations. TreeMap: backed by Red-Black Tree. Maintains natural key order (or custom Comparator). O(log n) get/put. Implements NavigableMap — provides floorKey(), ceilingKey(), subMap().
strongAnswerPoints: HashMap O(1), LinkedHashMap O(1) with insertion-order iteration, TreeMap O(log n) with sorted order.
TreeMap keys must implement Comparable or a Comparator must be provided.
LinkedHashMap can be used as an LRU cache by overriding removeEldestEntry.
None of the three are thread-safe — use ConcurrentHashMap or Collections.synchronizedMap() for concurrency.
whatToAvoid: Don't use TreeMap when you just need fast lookup — the O(log n) overhead is unnecessary unless you need sorted traversal.
ytQuery: HashMap vs LinkedHashMap vs TreeMap Java
quickAnswer: HashMap: fastest, no order. LinkedHashMap: insertion order preserved. TreeMap: sorted by key, O(log n). Pick by ordering need, not just performance.
eli10: HashMap is a messy desk — fast to grab things but no order. LinkedHashMap is a to-do list — you find things in the order you added them. TreeMap is a dictionary — everything sorted A-Z.
realWorldExample: Zomato uses LinkedHashMap for recently viewed restaurants (insertion order = recency). A leaderboard uses TreeMap to keep scores sorted without manual sorting on every update.

question: What is the difference between Comparable and Comparator in Java?
summary: Comparable: natural order in the class. Comparator: external custom order.
whatTheyTest: Understanding of sorting strategy pattern in Java.
explanation: Comparable (java.lang): the class implements it to define its natural ordering. compareTo() method. One ordering per class. Comparator (java.util): external object defining a comparison strategy. compare() method. Multiple Comparators can exist for the same class. Used in Collections.sort(list, comparator) and TreeMap/TreeSet constructors. Java 8: Comparator.comparing(), thenComparing(), reversed() for fluent chaining.
strongAnswerPoints: Comparable is intrusive — modifies the class itself. Comparator is non-intrusive.
Comparable defines ONE natural order; Comparator allows MANY orderings.
Java 8 Comparator.comparing() with method references is the idiomatic modern way.
TreeSet and TreeMap use Comparable by default; pass a Comparator to the constructor for custom order.
whatToAvoid: Don't manually implement compareTo with subtraction (return a - b) for integers — overflow risk. Use Integer.compare(a, b).
ytQuery: Comparable vs Comparator Java difference examples
quickAnswer: Comparable: class defines its own natural order (compareTo). Comparator: external strategy for custom ordering (compare). Use Comparable for the obvious single sort order; use Comparator for multiple or external sort strategies.
eli10: Comparable is like a person who already knows they're taller or shorter than someone (built-in). Comparator is like a judge at a competition who decides the ranking based on specific rules you give them.
realWorldExample: Meesho's Product class implements Comparable by price (natural order). But the UI offers sort by rating, name, or discount — each a separate Comparator passed to the sort function.

question: What is ConcurrentHashMap and how is it different from HashMap and Hashtable?
summary: ConcurrentHashMap: thread-safe, segment/node-level locking, no null keys. Better than Hashtable.
whatTheyTest: Thread-safety in collections and concurrent programming knowledge.
explanation: HashMap: not thread-safe. Multiple threads can corrupt the map (infinite loop in Java 7 during resize). Hashtable: thread-safe via synchronized on every method — one thread at a time for all operations. Very slow. ConcurrentHashMap: thread-safe with fine-grained locking. Java 7: 16 segments, each with its own lock. Java 8: CAS (Compare-And-Swap) operations + synchronized only on individual buckets during structural changes. Allows concurrent reads and concurrent writes to different buckets. Null keys and null values NOT allowed (ambiguity with get() returning null).
strongAnswerPoints: ConcurrentHashMap allows concurrent reads without locking — reads are non-blocking.
Java 8 uses CAS + bucket-level sync instead of segment locks — better scalability.
Hashtable is legacy; ConcurrentHashMap is the modern replacement.
putIfAbsent, compute, merge are atomic operations — use these instead of check-then-act.
whatToAvoid: Don't use synchronized(map) { map.get(); map.put(); } with ConcurrentHashMap — compound operations still need atomic methods like computeIfAbsent.
ytQuery: ConcurrentHashMap vs HashMap vs Hashtable Java thread safety
quickAnswer: HashMap: not thread-safe. Hashtable: thread-safe but method-level lock, slow. ConcurrentHashMap: thread-safe with bucket/CAS-level locking — reads don't lock, high concurrency. No null keys/values.
eli10: HashMap is a shared notebook with no rules — two people writing at once causes chaos. Hashtable puts a lock on the whole book — only one person at a time. ConcurrentHashMap splits the book into sections — multiple people can write in different sections simultaneously.
realWorldExample: CRED's transaction counter uses ConcurrentHashMap.merge(userId, 1, Integer::sum) for atomic per-user increments without explicit synchronization across 10k concurrent requests.

question: What are lambda expressions in Java 8? Explain syntax and functional interfaces.
summary: Lambdas: anonymous functions. Require @FunctionalInterface — one abstract method.
whatTheyTest: Java 8 functional programming knowledge.
explanation: A lambda is an anonymous function: (parameters) -> body. Requires a functional interface — an interface with exactly one abstract method (SAR: Single Abstract method Rule). @FunctionalInterface annotation enforces this. Built-in functional interfaces: Runnable (no args, no return), Supplier<T> (no args, returns T), Consumer<T> (takes T, no return), Function<T,R> (takes T, returns R), Predicate<T> (takes T, returns boolean), BiFunction<T,U,R>.
strongAnswerPoints: Lambdas enable passing behaviour as data — enables functional programming patterns.
@FunctionalInterface enforces the single abstract method rule at compile time.
Lambdas capture effectively final variables from enclosing scope.
Method references (Class::method) are shorthand lambdas for existing methods.
whatToAvoid: Don't mutate local variables inside a lambda — they must be effectively final. Don't use lambdas for complex multi-statement logic — extract to a method for readability.
ytQuery: Java 8 lambda expressions tutorial functional interface
quickAnswer: Lambda = anonymous function for functional interfaces. Syntax: (params) -> body. Requires interface with one abstract method. Built-ins: Predicate, Function, Consumer, Supplier. Enables clean Stream API usage.
eli10: A lambda is like a sticky note with instructions. Instead of writing a whole letter (anonymous class), you write a quick note: 'if name is long, print it'. You pass the note to whoever needs it.
realWorldExample: Swiggy's order filter pipeline uses Predicate lambdas: orders.stream().filter(o -> o.getStatus() == PENDING).filter(o -> o.getAmount() > 500).collect(toList()) — clean, readable, no anonymous class boilerplate.

question: Explain the Stream API in Java 8. What are intermediate and terminal operations?
summary: Streams: lazy pipeline of operations on data. Intermediate = lazy, terminal = triggers execution.
whatTheyTest: Functional programming with Java 8 Streams and lazy evaluation.
explanation: Stream represents a sequence of elements with a pipeline of operations. Key traits: 1) Lazy — intermediate operations don't execute until a terminal operation is called. 2) Consumed once — can't reuse a stream. 3) No storage — data flows through. Intermediate operations (return Stream): filter(), map(), flatMap(), distinct(), sorted(), limit(), skip(), peek(). Terminal operations (trigger execution, return value): forEach(), collect(), count(), reduce(), findFirst(), anyMatch(), toArray().
strongAnswerPoints: Lazy evaluation means filter/map chains don't run until collect/forEach is called — enables short-circuiting.
flatMap flattens Stream<Stream<T>> to Stream<T> — essential for nested collections.
Collectors.groupingBy, partitioningBy, joining are powerful terminal collectors.
Parallel streams (parallelStream()) split work across ForkJoinPool — use carefully for stateless operations only.
whatToAvoid: Don't use streams for simple loops — readability matters. Don't use parallel streams for I/O-bound work or when order matters without careful handling.
ytQuery: Java 8 Stream API tutorial intermediate terminal operations
quickAnswer: Streams are lazy pipelines. Intermediate ops (filter, map, sorted) return Stream and don't execute. Terminal ops (collect, forEach, count) trigger the pipeline. Data flows through without storage.
eli10: Stream is like a factory assembly line. Each station (filter, map) just sets up what to do. Nothing actually moves until the final station (collect) starts the belt. And you can only run the belt once.
realWorldExample: Flipkart's price aggregation: products.stream().filter(p -> p.inStock()).mapToDouble(Product::getPrice).average() — calculates average in-stock price without intermediate collections, saving memory on millions of SKUs.

question: What is Optional in Java 8? When and how should you use it?
summary: Optional wraps a potentially null value, forcing explicit null handling without NPE.
whatTheyTest: Null safety patterns and idiomatic Java 8 code.
explanation: Optional<T> is a container that may or may not contain a non-null value. Designed for method return types to signal that the result might be absent, instead of returning null. Key methods: of() (throws NPE if null), ofNullable() (accepts null), empty(), isPresent(), isEmpty(), get() (throws if empty), orElse(default), orElseGet(Supplier), orElseThrow(Supplier), map(), flatMap(), filter(), ifPresent(Consumer).
strongAnswerPoints: Use Optional as a return type, never as a field, parameter, or collection element.
orElseGet is lazy — prefer it over orElse when the default is expensive to create.
map() transforms the inner value; flatMap() unwraps nested Optional<Optional<T>>.
Don't call get() without isPresent() check — it's just a wrapped NPE.
whatToAvoid: Don't overuse Optional — it adds overhead. Don't use as method parameter or class field (serialization issues). Don't use Optional.get() without isPresent() check.
ytQuery: Java 8 Optional orElse orElseGet map flatMap
quickAnswer: Optional wraps nullable return values to force callers to handle absence explicitly. Use orElse for cheap defaults, orElseGet for expensive ones, orElseThrow for mandatory values, map/flatMap for transformations.
eli10: Optional is like a gift box that may or may not have something inside. Instead of opening it and maybe finding nothing (NPE), you first check — 'if there's something, use it; otherwise use this backup gift'.
realWorldExample: Freshworks' user lookup returns Optional<User> from the database layer. The service layer uses .orElseThrow(() -> new UserNotFoundException()) — eliminating null checks and making the absent-user case explicit in the API contract.

question: What are the four types of method references in Java 8?
summary: Static, instance (specific), instance (arbitrary), and constructor references.
whatTheyTest: Java 8 syntax fluency and functional programming.
explanation: Method references are shorthand lambdas that refer to existing methods. Four types: 1) Static method reference: ClassName::staticMethod — e.g., Integer::parseInt. 2) Instance method of a specific object: instance::method — e.g., myPrinter::print. 3) Instance method of an arbitrary object of a type: ClassName::instanceMethod — e.g., String::toUpperCase (the instance is the first parameter). 4) Constructor reference: ClassName::new — e.g., ArrayList::new.
strongAnswerPoints: Method references are more readable than equivalent lambdas when the lambda just calls a method.
Type 3 (arbitrary instance) is the most common — String::length, String::toUpperCase.
Constructor references enable factory patterns without explicit lambda boilerplate.
All method references are syntactic sugar — they compile to the same bytecode as equivalent lambdas.
whatToAvoid: Don't force method references when the lambda is clearer. e -> e.getAge() > 18 is clearer than an obscure method reference chain.
ytQuery: Java 8 method references four types explained
quickAnswer: 4 types: 1) ClassName::staticMethod 2) instance::method 3) ClassName::instanceMethod (arbitrary instance) 4) ClassName::new (constructor). All are shorthand for equivalent lambdas.
eli10: Method references are like giving someone a phone book entry instead of writing out their full address. Instead of saying 'go to 42 Java Street, ring the bell, ask for parseInt', you just say 'call Integer::parseInt'.
realWorldExample: Zomato's restaurant pipeline: restaurants.stream().map(Restaurant::getName).sorted().forEach(System.out::println) — three method references, zero verbose lambdas.

question: What is a Java Record (Java 16)? How does it differ from a regular class?
summary: Record: immutable data carrier. Auto-generates constructor, getters, equals, hashCode, toString.
whatTheyTest: Awareness of modern Java features and boilerplate reduction.
explanation: Records (Java 16, stable) are a special kind of class for modeling immutable data. Declaring record Point(int x, int y) {} automatically generates: a canonical constructor, private final fields, public accessor methods (x(), y() — not getX()), equals(), hashCode(), and toString(). Records are implicitly final, cannot extend other classes, and all components are final. Can implement interfaces, have static fields/methods, and compact constructors for validation.
strongAnswerPoints: Records eliminate boilerplate for data-carrying classes — equals/hashCode/toString are correct by default.
Components are accessed as x() not getX() — a breaking change from JavaBean conventions.
Records cannot be extended (implicitly final) and cannot extend other classes.
Use records for DTOs, value objects, API responses — anywhere you'd write an immutable data class.
whatToAvoid: Don't use records for mutable domain objects or entities with identity — records define equality by component values, not identity.
ytQuery: Java 16 Records tutorial immutable class
quickAnswer: record Point(int x, int y) {} auto-generates constructor, final fields, accessors (x() not getX()), equals, hashCode, toString. Immutable, final, zero boilerplate. Perfect for DTOs and value objects.
eli10: A record is like a name tag. You say 'this person has a name and age' and Java automatically makes it so you can read those — no need to write all the boring get-name, equals, print stuff yourself.
realWorldExample: Razorpay's API response layer replaced 20-field DTO classes with records: record PaymentResponse(String id, double amount, String currency, String status) {} — cut boilerplate by 80%.

question: What is the difference between Thread, Runnable, and Callable in Java?
summary: Thread: concrete class. Runnable: no return. Callable: returns value, throws checked exception.
whatTheyTest: Concurrent programming fundamentals and task submission patterns.
explanation: Thread: a class you can extend (or compose with Runnable). Runnable: @FunctionalInterface with run() — no return value, no checked exceptions. Submit to ExecutorService.execute(). Callable<V>: @FunctionalInterface with call() — returns V, can throw checked exceptions. Submit to ExecutorService.submit() which returns Future<V>. Use Callable when you need the result or need to propagate exceptions from the task.
strongAnswerPoints: Callable returns a value and can throw checked exceptions; Runnable cannot do either.
submit() returns Future<V>; execute() returns void.
Future.get() is blocking — use timeout overload to avoid indefinite waits.
Prefer ExecutorService over raw Thread.start() — better resource management.
whatToAvoid: Don't call future.get() on the main thread without a timeout — it blocks indefinitely if the task hangs.
ytQuery: Java Thread Runnable Callable difference ExecutorService Future
quickAnswer: Runnable: no return, no checked exception (execute()). Callable<V>: returns V, can throw checked exception (submit() → Future<V>). Thread is the class; both Runnable and Callable are task definitions submitted to it or an executor.
eli10: Runnable is like giving someone a chore to do with no report back. Callable is like asking someone to go buy something and bring back a receipt. Thread is the person doing it. ExecutorService is the hiring agency that manages many people.
realWorldExample: PhonePe's payment processor uses Callable<PaymentResult> tasks submitted to a thread pool. The Future is checked after a 5-second timeout — if expired, the payment is marked 'pending' and retried.

question: What is the `synchronized` keyword in Java? Method-level vs block-level synchronization.
summary: synchronized acquires the object monitor lock, ensuring only one thread executes the guarded code.
whatTheyTest: Thread safety and mutual exclusion in Java.
explanation: synchronized uses the object's intrinsic monitor lock. synchronized method: locks the entire object (this) for the duration of the method — coarse-grained. static synchronized: locks the Class object. synchronized block: locks a specified object for only the critical section — finer grained, better throughput. Any code NOT in the synchronized block can still run concurrently. Prefer smaller synchronized blocks to reduce contention.
strongAnswerPoints: synchronized method locks 'this'; static synchronized locks the Class object.
Block-level sync on a private final lock object is safer and more performant.
Synchronized methods of the same object are mutually exclusive with each other.
synchronized doesn't protect against visibility issues on other objects — use volatile or atomic types for simple flags.
whatToAvoid: Don't synchronize on a non-final or publicly accessible object — another thread could lock on the same object externally, causing deadlocks.
ytQuery: Java synchronized keyword method block level difference
quickAnswer: synchronized acquires the object monitor. Method-level locks 'this' (coarse). Block-level locks a specific object for just the critical section (fine-grained, better performance). static synchronized locks the Class object.
eli10: synchronized is like a bathroom with one key. Method-level = locking the whole house (wasteful). Block-level = just locking the bathroom door (smart). Only one person can be in the bathroom at a time.
realWorldExample: A bank balance update uses synchronized block on the account object: synchronized(account) { account.debit(amount); } — only that account is locked, allowing other accounts to be processed concurrently.

question: What is CompletableFuture in Java 8? How is it different from Future?
summary: CompletableFuture: non-blocking async chaining. Future: blocking get(). CF = async pipeline.
whatTheyTest: Asynchronous programming patterns in Java.
explanation: Future<V>: represents an async computation. get() is blocking — thread waits. No chaining. No manual completion. Cannot combine multiple futures easily. CompletableFuture<V>: implements Future + CompletionStage. Non-blocking callbacks: thenApply (transform), thenAccept (consume), thenRun (no input/output), thenCompose (chain async), thenCombine (combine two). allOf() waits for all; anyOf() waits for first. Can be manually completed with complete(). Uses ForkJoinPool.commonPool() by default or a custom Executor.
strongAnswerPoints: CompletableFuture callbacks (thenApply etc.) run on the completing thread or common pool — no blocking.
thenApply = map (sync transform); thenCompose = flatMap (async chaining).
allOf() returns CF<Void>; use thenApply to extract results from individual futures.
exceptionally() handles errors in the pipeline — equivalent to catch in async chain.
whatToAvoid: Don't call cf.get() in a callback — it defeats the non-blocking purpose. Don't use the common ForkJoinPool for I/O tasks — pass a dedicated executor.
ytQuery: Java CompletableFuture vs Future async programming
quickAnswer: Future.get() blocks. CompletableFuture chains non-blocking callbacks: thenApply (transform), thenCompose (async chain), thenCombine (merge two), allOf (wait for all), exceptionally (error handling). Full async pipeline without blocking threads.
eli10: Future is like ordering food and standing at the counter waiting. CompletableFuture is like ordering and getting a buzzer — you go sit down, do other things, and the buzzer goes off when it's ready. You can even say 'when food's ready, add ketchup, then take it to table 5'.
realWorldExample: Razorpay's payment flow: fetchUser, fetchMerchant, and checkFraud run in parallel with CompletableFuture.allOf() — then combine results. Reduced payment initiation latency from 900ms (sequential) to 350ms (parallel).

question: What is a deadlock in Java? How do you detect and prevent it?
summary: Deadlock: two threads each hold a lock the other needs. Prevent by consistent lock ordering.
whatTheyTest: Deep concurrency knowledge and ability to reason about thread interactions.
explanation: Deadlock: Thread A holds lock1, waits for lock2. Thread B holds lock2, waits for lock1. Neither can proceed. Four conditions (all must hold): Mutual exclusion, Hold and wait, No preemption, Circular wait. Prevention: 1) Consistent lock ordering (always acquire locks in the same order). 2) Use tryLock() with timeout (ReentrantLock). 3) Lock only what's needed. Detection: thread dump (jstack), VisualVM, ThreadMXBean.findDeadlockedThreads().
strongAnswerPoints: Deadlock requires all four Coffman conditions — breaking any one prevents it.
Consistent lock ordering eliminates circular wait — the most practical prevention.
ReentrantLock.tryLock() with timeout lets you back off instead of waiting forever.
jstack <pid> prints thread dumps and identifies deadlocked threads.
whatToAvoid: Don't hold locks while calling external APIs, doing I/O, or calling unknown code — you lose control over what other locks might be acquired.
ytQuery: Java deadlock detection prevention example
quickAnswer: Deadlock: circular lock dependency. Thread A holds lock1 needs lock2; Thread B holds lock2 needs lock1. Prevent: acquire locks in consistent global order. Use tryLock with timeout. Minimize lock scope. Detect: jstack thread dump.
eli10: Two kids each have one lego piece the other needs. Kid A won't give theirs until they get Kid B's piece. Kid B won't give theirs until they get Kid A's. Nobody moves. That's a deadlock. The fix: agree that everyone asks for the red piece first, always.
realWorldExample: A bank transfer deadlock: transferAtoB locks accountA then accountB. Simultaneously transferBtoA locks accountB then accountA. Fixed by always locking the lower account ID first — consistent ordering regardless of transfer direction.

question: What is the volatile keyword in Java and when should you use it?
summary: volatile guarantees visibility across threads — reads/writes go directly to main memory.
whatTheyTest: Java memory model and cache visibility issues in concurrent programming.
explanation: Without volatile, threads may cache variable values in CPU registers/caches. One thread's update may not be visible to another. volatile forces all reads to come from main memory and all writes to go to main memory immediately. Also establishes a happens-before relationship. But volatile is NOT atomic for compound operations like count++. Use for simple flags, singleton double-checked locking, and status variables read by multiple threads.
strongAnswerPoints: volatile guarantees visibility, NOT atomicity. count++ with volatile is still not thread-safe.
For atomic operations use AtomicInteger, AtomicReference or synchronized.
volatile creates a happens-before relationship — all writes before the volatile write are visible after the volatile read.
Singleton double-checked locking requires volatile to prevent instruction reordering.
whatToAvoid: Don't use volatile as a replacement for synchronized when you need atomicity of compound operations (check-then-act, read-modify-write).
ytQuery: Java volatile keyword memory visibility explained
quickAnswer: volatile forces reads/writes to go to main memory — ensures visibility across threads. Not atomic — count++ with volatile is still a race condition. Use for flags, DCL singleton. Use AtomicInteger for counters.
eli10: Without volatile, each worker has a personal notepad where they write values. The boss updates the main board but workers might still read their old notepad. volatile says 'always look at the main board, never your notepad'.
realWorldExample: A background thread monitors a volatile boolean shuttingDown flag. When the main thread sets shuttingDown = true, all worker threads (reading the volatile) immediately see the change and clean up gracefully.

question: What are the JVM memory areas? Explain Heap, Stack, Method Area, and PC Register.
summary: JVM memory: Heap (objects), Stack (frames), Method Area (class metadata), PC Register (current instruction).
whatTheyTest: JVM internals and memory management understanding.
explanation: Heap: shared across all threads. Stores objects and class instances. Garbage collected. Divided into Young Generation (Eden + Survivor spaces) and Old Generation. Method Area (Metaspace in Java 8+): stores class metadata, static variables, constant pool, method bytecode. Shared across threads. Stack: per-thread. Stores frames — local variables, operand stack, return address. One frame per method call. Grows with call depth — StackOverflowError when full. PC Register: per-thread. Points to the current JVM instruction being executed. Native Method Stack: for native (C/C++) method calls.
strongAnswerPoints: Heap is shared, garbage collected, stores objects. Stack is per-thread, stores frames.
Stack is fast (push/pop); Heap is flexible but needs GC.
Metaspace (Java 8) replaced PermGen — no fixed size limit by default.
StackOverflowError = stack too deep. OutOfMemoryError = heap or Metaspace full.
whatToAvoid: Don't confuse Stack overflow (too many nested calls) with OutOfMemoryError (too many objects on heap). They're different memory areas.
ytQuery: JVM memory areas heap stack method area explained
quickAnswer: Heap: shared, objects, GC'd. Stack: per-thread, method frames, local vars. Metaspace: class metadata, static fields. PC Register: current instruction per thread. StackOverflowError = stack. OutOfMemoryError = heap.
eli10: JVM memory is like a workplace. Heap is the shared storage room (objects live here). Stack is each worker's personal desk (current work goes here). Method Area is the filing cabinet with instruction manuals. PC Register is each worker's sticky note showing which step they're on.
realWorldExample: Flipkart's microservice tuned with -Xmx4g for the catalog service (millions of Product objects on heap) but -Xss256k for the API layer (many threads, shallow call stacks) — right-sizing each memory area cut GC pause times by 40%.

question: How does Garbage Collection work in Java? Explain generational GC and G1GC.
summary: GC reclaims unreachable objects. Generational GC: Young/Old gen. G1GC: region-based, low pause.
whatTheyTest: JVM internals, performance tuning, and GC knowledge.
explanation: GC identifies unreachable objects (not reachable from GC roots: thread stacks, static fields) and reclaims their memory. Generational hypothesis: most objects die young. Heap split into: Young Generation (Eden + S0 + S1 Survivor spaces) — minor GC, fast. Old Generation — major/full GC, slow, stop-the-world. Objects surviving enough minor GCs get promoted to Old Gen (tenured). G1GC (Java 9+ default): divides heap into equal-sized regions. Collects regions with most garbage first. Aims for configurable pause time targets. Concurrent marking reduces STW pauses. ZGC and Shenandoah: sub-millisecond pause GCs for latency-sensitive apps.
strongAnswerPoints: Minor GC (Young Gen) is fast and frequent; Major GC (Old Gen) is slow and causes longer STW pauses.
G1GC replaces CMS; ZGC and Shenandoah achieve sub-millisecond pauses for very large heaps.
Memory leaks in Java come from unintended strong references — static collections, listeners not removed.
Use WeakReference for caches — allows GC to reclaim when memory is tight.
whatToAvoid: Don't call System.gc() — it's a hint, not guaranteed. Don't assume GC is free — GC pauses impact latency. Don't ignore GC logs when debugging performance.
ytQuery: Java Garbage Collection G1GC generational explained
quickAnswer: GC reclaims unreachable objects. Young Gen (minor GC, fast). Old Gen (major GC, slow). G1GC: region-based, targets configurable pause times. ZGC: sub-ms pauses. Memory leaks = strong references keeping objects alive unintentionally.
eli10: GC is like a janitor who comes around and cleans up forgotten toys. Young area (Eden) is the playroom — cleaned often because toys are left there all the time. Old area is the closet — cleaned less often but takes longer. G1GC is a smarter janitor who starts with the messiest rooms first.
realWorldExample: Swiggy's order tracking service had 2-second GC pauses causing timeouts. Switching from CMS to G1GC with -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200 reduced max pauses to under 200ms, eliminating order tracking timeouts during peak hours.

question: Why is String immutable in Java? What is the String Pool?
summary: String immutable for security, caching, thread safety. Pool deduplicates string literals.
whatTheyTest: Java fundamentals and understanding of String internals.
explanation: String is immutable (final class, private final char[] value) because: 1) Security: used as class names, DB URLs, passwords — mutability would be a security risk. 2) String Pool (interning): JVM can safely cache and share String literals. Same literal content → same object in pool. 3) Thread safety: immutable objects need no synchronization. 4) Hash caching: hashCode is computed once and cached — efficient as HashMap keys. String pool: area in Heap (Metaspace pre-Java 7) where String literals are interned. new String() bypasses the pool. String.intern() forces pool lookup/entry.
strongAnswerPoints: String immutability enables safe sharing in the String pool and as HashMap keys.
== compares references; equals() compares content — always use equals() for Strings.
String concatenation in loops creates O(n²) intermediate Strings — use StringBuilder.
Java 7+: String pool moved to main Heap — subject to GC, eliminates OutOfMemoryError from pool overflow.
whatToAvoid: Never use == to compare String values. Don't use String concatenation (+) inside loops — use StringBuilder.
ytQuery: Java String immutable String pool intern explained
quickAnswer: String is immutable for security, caching (pool), thread safety, and hash caching. String pool stores interned literals — same literal reuses same object. Use equals() not == for comparison. StringBuilder for string building.
eli10: String pool is like a shared nickname list. If two people both go by 'Java', instead of writing their name twice, we write it once and point both people to it. Since nobody can change a name on the list, sharing is safe.
realWorldExample: HashMap<String, Config> uses String keys heavily. Because String caches its hashCode, repeated map lookups with the same key are faster — the hash is computed once and reused, critical for high-throughput config lookups.

question: What is the difference between checked and unchecked exceptions in Java?
summary: Checked: must handle at compile time. Unchecked (RuntimeException): optional handling.
whatTheyTest: Exception handling design and Java's exception hierarchy.
explanation: Checked exceptions: extend Exception directly. Compiler forces you to either catch or declare with throws. Represent recoverable conditions (IOException, SQLException). Unchecked exceptions: extend RuntimeException. Compiler doesn't require handling. Represent programming errors or unrecoverable conditions (NullPointerException, IllegalArgumentException, ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException). Error: extends Throwable directly. JVM-level issues not meant to be caught (OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError). Modern trend: prefer unchecked exceptions — checked exceptions hurt API design and force try-catch boilerplate.
strongAnswerPoints: Checked: compiler-enforced recovery (IOException, SQLException). Unchecked: programming errors (NPE, IllegalArgumentException).
Spring and most modern frameworks use unchecked exceptions for cleaner APIs.
Always include context in exception messages — 'User not found: 42' beats 'Not found'.
try-with-resources (Java 7+) ensures AutoCloseable resources are closed — prefer over finally blocks.
whatToAvoid: Don't catch Exception or Throwable broadly and swallow it silently. Don't use checked exceptions for conditions the caller can't reasonably recover from.
ytQuery: Java checked vs unchecked exceptions RuntimeException
quickAnswer: Checked (IOException, SQLException): compiler forces catch or throws declaration. Unchecked (RuntimeException, NPE, IllegalArgument): no forced handling. Error: JVM-level, don't catch. Modern Java prefers unchecked for cleaner API design.
eli10: Checked exceptions are like warnings on a form you have to sign before proceeding. Unchecked exceptions are like unexpected crashes — you didn't sign anything, things just went wrong. Errors are like a power outage — there's nothing you can realistically do.
realWorldExample: Razorpay wraps JDBC SQLExceptions (checked) in DataAccessException (unchecked) in the DAO layer — preventing checked exception leakage into the service layer and keeping business logic clean.

question: What is ExecutorService in Java? Explain thread pool types.
summary: ExecutorService manages thread pools. Fixed, cached, scheduled, single, work-stealing pools.
whatTheyTest: Thread pool management and concurrent task execution.
explanation: ExecutorService decouples task submission from thread management. Thread pools reuse threads — avoid overhead of creating new threads per task. Types: newFixedThreadPool(n): fixed n threads, unbounded queue. Backpressure: tasks queue if all busy. newCachedThreadPool(): creates threads on demand, reuses idle ones, idle > 60s → terminate. Good for short-lived tasks. newSingleThreadExecutor(): single thread, tasks serialized. newScheduledThreadPool(n): for delayed/periodic tasks. newWorkStealingPool(): ForkJoinPool-based, work stealing, parallel tasks. ThreadPoolExecutor: fully configurable with core/max pool size, queue type, rejection policy.
strongAnswerPoints: newCachedThreadPool creates unbounded threads — risky under heavy load; can cause OutOfMemoryError.
newFixedThreadPool with unbounded queue can cause OOM if queue grows indefinitely.
Always shutdown() the executor service to release threads.
ThreadPoolExecutor.CallerRunsPolicy provides backpressure — slows the caller instead of dropping tasks.
whatToAvoid: Never use Executors.newCachedThreadPool() for high-throughput server workloads — unbounded thread creation. Don't ignore rejected task exceptions.
ytQuery: Java ExecutorService thread pool types FixedThreadPool CachedThreadPool
quickAnswer: ExecutorService manages thread pools. Fixed: n threads, queue tasks. Cached: dynamic threads, short-lived tasks. Single: serial execution. Scheduled: delayed/periodic. Use ThreadPoolExecutor for fine-grained control of queue type and rejection policy.
eli10: ExecutorService is like a staffing agency. Fixed pool = always 4 workers, extra work waits in line. Cached pool = hire as many as needed but fire idle workers after a minute. Scheduled pool = workers who come in at specific times.
realWorldExample: CRED's notification service uses a fixed thread pool of 20 for sending push notifications. When the queue grows beyond 1000 during a campaign, CallerRunsPolicy kicks in — slowing the campaign sender instead of dropping notifications.

question: What is == vs .equals() vs Objects.equals() in Java?
summary: == compares references. equals() compares content. Objects.equals() is null-safe.
whatTheyTest: Java fundamentals — reference vs value equality.
explanation: == for primitives: compares values. For objects: compares memory addresses (references). equals(): Object's default equals() is == (reference). Overriding equals() defines value equality. String, Integer, List override it. hashCode() contract: if a.equals(b) then a.hashCode() == b.hashCode(). Objects.equals(a, b): null-safe wrapper — returns false if either is null (no NPE), calls a.equals(b) otherwise. Always override both equals() and hashCode() together.
strongAnswerPoints: Integer cache: -128 to 127 are cached — == works by coincidence. Above 127, == fails even with same value.
Always use equals() for object content comparison, never == (except intentional reference check).
Objects.equals() is the safest — handles null on both sides without NPE.
If you override equals(), you MUST override hashCode() — required by the contract.
whatToAvoid: Don't use == to compare Strings or any wrapper types. Don't rely on Integer == for values outside the cached range.
ytQuery: Java == vs equals vs Objects.equals null safe
quickAnswer: ==: reference equality (same object in memory). equals(): content equality (overridden in String, Integer etc). Objects.equals(): null-safe content equality. Always use equals() for object comparison. Always override equals + hashCode together.
eli10: == is like asking 'are these the exact same house key (same key, from the same keychain)'. equals() is like asking 'do these two keys open the same lock (same pattern)'. Objects.equals is smart enough to handle it if one key doesn't exist yet.
realWorldExample: A bug at Paytm: a transaction deduplication check used transactionId1 == transactionId2 on String IDs — worked in dev (small IDs, pooled) but failed in prod with longer IDs (new String objects). Fixed by switching to equals().

question: What is the fail-fast vs fail-safe iterator in Java?
summary: Fail-fast: throws ConcurrentModificationException on modification during iteration. Fail-safe: iterates a copy.
whatTheyTest: Iterator internals and concurrent modification behavior.
explanation: Fail-fast iterator (ArrayList, HashMap, HashSet): checks modCount field on each iteration step. If collection structurally modified during iteration (add/remove — not set) by any means other than the iterator's own remove(), throws ConcurrentModificationException. Not guaranteed to throw — best-effort. Fail-safe iterator (CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap, Collections.unmodifiableCollection): iterates over a copy or snapshot. No ConcurrentModificationException. May not reflect recent changes. Higher memory cost.
strongAnswerPoints: Fail-fast uses modCount — incremented on structural changes, checked on each next() call.
Never rely on ConcurrentModificationException — it's not guaranteed to fire.
Iterator.remove() and List.removeIf() are the correct ways to remove during iteration.
CopyOnWriteArrayList is fail-safe but expensive for write-heavy workloads — each write copies the array.
whatToAvoid: Don't modify a collection in an enhanced for loop — use Iterator.remove(), removeIf(), or stream filter to a new collection.
ytQuery: Java fail-fast fail-safe iterator ConcurrentModificationException
quickAnswer: Fail-fast (ArrayList, HashMap): throws ConcurrentModificationException if modified during iteration. Fail-safe (CopyOnWriteArrayList, ConcurrentHashMap): iterates over a snapshot, no exception. Use Iterator.remove() or removeIf() for safe removal.
eli10: Fail-fast is like a strict librarian who notices if you rearrange books while she's cataloguing — she stops and yells. Fail-safe is like a librarian working from a photocopy of the catalog — she doesn't notice changes but never gets confused.
realWorldExample: A Zomato bug: restaurant list was modified (new restaurant added) while iterating to build the search index — ConcurrentModificationException in prod. Fixed with removeIf() for filtering and CopyOnWriteArrayList for the read-heavy restaurant registry.

question: What are Generics in Java? What is type erasure?
summary: Generics: compile-time type safety. Type erasure removes generic info at runtime.
whatTheyTest: Understanding of Java's type system and compile-time vs runtime type info.
explanation: Generics enable parameterized types — write code once that works for any type, with compile-time type checking. Without generics, collections required casts and risked ClassCastException at runtime. Type erasure: the compiler removes generic type information at compile time and replaces with Object (or bounds). The JVM has no knowledge of generic types at runtime — List<String> and List<Integer> are the same List.class at runtime. Wildcards: <?> (unknown), <? extends T> (upper bounded, read), <? super T> (lower bounded, write).
strongAnswerPoints: Type erasure means you can't do: new T() or T.class or instanceof T at runtime.
PECS: Producer Extends, Consumer Super — guideline for wildcard choice.
Bounded generics <T extends Comparable<T>> enable calling methods of the bound type.
Generics are invariant: List<Dog> is NOT a List<Animal> even if Dog extends Animal.
whatToAvoid: Don't create generic arrays (new T[]) — type erasure makes it unsafe. Don't use raw types like List instead of List<String> — loses all type safety.
ytQuery: Java Generics type erasure wildcards explained
quickAnswer: Generics add compile-time type safety. Type erasure: JVM replaces generics with Object at runtime — no runtime type info. PECS: extends for reading, super for writing. List<Dog> ≠ List<Animal> (invariant).
eli10: Generics are like labelled boxes. Instead of throwing everything into an unlabelled box and guessing what's inside, you label it 'String box'. The label is checked when you pack it (compile time). But when the box is actually shipped (runtime), the label is removed — it's just a box.
realWorldExample: Freshworks' generic Repository<T, ID> interface: findById(ID id) returns Optional<T>. One interface for UserRepository, TicketRepository, ContactRepository — compile-time type safety without duplication.

question: What is the difference between wait(), sleep(), and yield() in Java?
summary: wait(): releases lock, waits for notify(). sleep(): keeps lock, pauses thread. yield(): hints to scheduler.
whatTheyTest: Thread lifecycle and synchronization primitives knowledge.
explanation: wait(): Object method. Must be in synchronized block. Releases the lock and pauses the thread until notify() or notifyAll() is called. Used for inter-thread communication. sleep(): Thread static method. Pauses thread for specified time. Does NOT release any locks held. Can throw InterruptedException. yield(): Thread static method. Hints to JVM scheduler to give other threads a chance to run. No guarantees — implementation-dependent. All three can be interrupted (InterruptedException).
strongAnswerPoints: wait() releases the lock; sleep() does not — critical difference for deadlock prevention.
wait() must be in a synchronized block; sleep() can be called anywhere.
Always call wait() in a while loop, not if — protect against spurious wakeups.
Restore interrupt flag after catching InterruptedException: Thread.currentThread().interrupt().
whatToAvoid: Don't call wait() in an if — use while to re-check the condition after wakeup (spurious wakeups). Don't sleep while holding a lock unnecessarily.
ytQuery: Java wait sleep yield difference synchronized notify
quickAnswer: wait(): releases lock, pauses until notify() — must be in synchronized block. sleep(): timed pause, keeps locks. yield(): hint to give up CPU. wait() is for coordination; sleep() is for delays; yield() is rarely useful.
eli10: wait() is like pausing a meeting and letting others use the conference room until someone calls you back. sleep() is like napping in the conference room — you're still holding it, nobody else can use it. yield() is politely stepping aside to let someone cut the line, but they might not take you up on it.
realWorldExample: A print spooler uses wait()/notifyAll() — printer thread waits when queue empty, document threads notifyAll() when new docs added. This is more efficient than sleep-polling the queue every 100ms.
